## ML: The basics

::: {.incremental style="line-height: 2;"}
- Loss function: measures model error
- Gradient descent: moves weights down the loss surface
- Backpropagation: computes gradients layer by layer
- Overfitting: memorises training data, fails to generalise
:::

## ML: The basics

:::{style="display: block; margin: 0 auto; text-align: center; margin-top: 2em;"}
```{mermaid}
%%| fig-height: 5
%%{init: {"flowchart": {"nodeSpacing": 15, "rankSpacing": 18}, "theme": "base", "themeVariables": {"edgeLabelBackground": "#ffffff"}}}%%
flowchart TD
    A([Training data]) --> B["Forward pass"]
    B --> C[Compute loss]
    C --> D[Backpropagation]
    D --> E[Update weights]
    E -->|repeat| B
    C -->|loss small enough| F([Done ✓])

    classDef io     fill:#6c757d,stroke:#495057,color:#fff
    classDef step   fill:#4b9cd3,stroke:#2c6e9e,color:#fff
    classDef key    fill:#003b6f,stroke:#001f3f,color:#fff

    class A,F io
    class B,C step
    class D,E key
```
:::

::: {.notes}
- Loss function: measures model error
- Gradient descent: moves weights down the loss surface
- Backpropagation: computes gradients layer by layer
- Overfitting: memorises training data, fails to generalise

Overfitting is a major concern in classical ML, but large transformer models largely sidestep it. They have billions of parameters (far more than training examples), yet generalise well. Key reasons: (1) vast and diverse training data makes true memorisation impractical; (2) regularisation techniques such as dropout and weight decay are baked in; (3) the double-descent phenomenon: very over-parameterised models can re-enter a good-generalisation regime; and (4) emergent representations learned across billions of tokens capture structure rather than surface patterns.
:::

## Transformers: Inference I {auto-animate=true}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](images/tf-steps-simple-1.png){width="70%" data-id="basic-inference-img"}
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
:::
::::

## Transformers: Inference I {auto-animate=true}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](images/tf-steps-simple-2.png){width="70%" data-id="basic-inference-img"}
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
::: {style="font-size: 0.8em; color: #555; margin-top: 4em;"}
Convert text to tokens
:::
:::
::::

## Transformers: Inference I {auto-animate=true}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](images/tf-steps-simple-3.png){width="70%" data-id="basic-inference-img"}
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
::: {style="font-size: 0.8em; color: #555; margin-top: 4em;"}
Convert text to tokens
:::
::: {style="font-size: 0.8em; color: #555; margin-top: 1.25em;"}
Predict new tokens
:::
:::
::::

## Transformers: Inference I {auto-animate=true}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](images/tf-steps-simple-4.png){width="70%" data-id="basic-inference-img"}
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
::: {style="font-size: 0.8em; color: #555; margin-top: 4em;"}
Convert text to tokens
:::
::: {style="font-size: 0.8em; color: #555; margin-top: 1.25em;"}
Predict new tokens
:::
::: {style="font-size: 0.8em; color: #555; margin-top: 1.25em;"}
Convert tokens to text
:::
:::
::::

::: {.notes}
- Tokenise: text to token IDs
- Generate: predict next tokens autoregressively
- Decode: token IDs to text
:::

## Transformers: Inference II {auto-animate=false}

![](images/tfp-step0a.png){width="40%" style="display: block; margin: 0 auto;" data-id="inference-img"}

## Transformers: Inference II {auto-animate=false}

![](images/tfp-step0b.png){width="40%" style="display: block; margin: 0 auto;" data-id="inference-img"}

## Transformers: Inference II {auto-animate=true}

![](images/tfp-step1-alternate.png){width="40%" style="display: block; margin: 0 auto;" data-id="inference-img"}

## Transformers: Inference II {auto-animate=true}

![](images/tfp-step2-alternate.png){width="40%" style="display: block; margin: 0 auto;" data-id="inference-img"}

## Transformers: Inference II {auto-animate=true}

![](images/tfp-step4-alternate.png){width="40%" style="display: block; margin: 0 auto;" data-id="inference-img"}

## Transformers: Inference II {auto-animate=true}

![](images/tfp-step5-alternate.png){width="40%" style="display: block; margin: 0 auto;" data-id="inference-img"}

## Transformers: Inference II {auto-animate=true}

![](images/tfp-step6-alternate.png){width="40%" style="display: block; margin: 0 auto;" data-id="inference-img"}

## Transformers: Inference II {auto-animate=true}

![](images/tfp-step7-alternate.png){width="40%" style="display: block; margin: 0 auto;" data-id="inference-img"}

::: {.notes}
- Tokenise: text to sub-word token IDs
- LLM: token IDs enter the model
- Embed + pos. encoding: token IDs to dense vectors with position
- Self-attention + MLP × N layers: every token attends to every other
- Predict & sample: logits to softmax to sample next token
- Autoregressive: feed token back, repeat
- Decode: token IDs to text

The crucial innovation is self-attention: rather than processing tokens in sequence (like an RNN), every token attends to every other token in parallel. This lets the model capture long-range dependencies and scales well on modern hardware.

Sampling strategies: the model doesn't always pick the most likely next token. Temperature scales the probability distribution (higher = more random). Top-k restricts sampling to the k most likely tokens. Top-p (nucleus sampling) samples from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p.
:::

## Embeddings encode meaning

::: {style="font-size: 0.85em;"}
The embedding matrix maps tokens to vectors; directions encode meaning.
:::

:::{style="text-align: center; margin-top: 0.3em;"}
![](./images/3b1b-embedding.png){width="52%"}
:::

::: {style="font-size: 0.75em; color:#555; margin-top: 0.3em;"}
Attention enriches each vector with context: bank (river) vs bank (finance)
:::

::: {.attribution}
3Blue1Brown, [Deep Learning Ch. 5](https://www.3blue1brown.com/lessons/gpt)
:::

::: {.notes}
The food / country direction generalises across many pairs: pizza / Italy, tacos / Mexico, sushi / Japan. These patterns exist before attention runs. Attention enriches each vector with context, distinguishing "bank" (river) from "bank" (finance).
:::

{{< include ./_call_code.qmd >}}

## Agentic AI

An AI Agents are essentially some scaffolding / loop around LLM(s):

::: {.incremental}
- LLM: The reasoning engine. It plans, evaluates, and decides whether to "act" or "answer."
- System Prompt: Defines the persona, available tools, and operational boundaries.
- Context Window: The Short-term Memory. Maintains the state, including history, tool outputs, and the current goal.
- Tools: External capabilities like Web Search, Python Code, APIs, or RAG.
:::

::: {.notes}
- LLM is like a goldfish - completely stateless. All short term memory lives in the context window. The "loop" is managed by the surrounding application code, not the model.

:::
## Agentic AI

:::{style="margin-top: 3em;"}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {"flowchart": {"nodeSpacing": 40, "rankSpacing": 80}, "theme": "base", "themeVariables": {"edgeLabelBackground": "#ffffff"}}}%%
flowchart LR
    S([System prompt]) --> C
    H([Prompt]) --> C["Context window"]
    C --> L["LLM: generate text"]
    L --> D{Tool call?}
    D -->|yes| T["Tools (incl. RAG)"]
    T -->|result appended| C
    D -->|no| O([Output])

    classDef io      fill:#6c757d,stroke:#495057,color:#fff
    classDef core    fill:#003b6f,stroke:#001f3f,color:#fff
    classDef support fill:#4b9cd3,stroke:#2c6e9e,color:#fff
    classDef decision fill:#e9c46a,stroke:#f4a261,color:#000

    class S,H,O io
    class L core
    class C,T support
    class D decision
```
:::

::: {.notes}
- LLM: reasons and decides to act or answer
- System prompt: defines role, tools, and behaviour
- Context window: holds all state (prompt, results, history)
- Planning: reason step-by-step before acting
- Tools: search, code, APIs, RAG
- Termination: stop when done or max_iterations reached

An agentic system wraps an LLM in a loop: the model receives a prompt, decides whether it needs more information or to take an action, calls a tool if so, gets the result back in its context, and repeats until it decides it is done.

The key components:
- LLM: does all the reasoning — it reads the context, decides what to do next, and generates either a tool call or a final answer.
- Tools: external capabilities the LLM can invoke — web search, a code interpreter, database queries, API calls. The LLM doesn't execute these itself; the surrounding framework does and appends the result to the context.
- Memory / RAG: retrieved documents or prior conversation history injected into the context before the LLM runs. This is how agents can work with knowledge beyond their training cutoff or access private data.
- Planning: some agents explicitly decompose a task into sub-steps before acting (ReAct, chain-of-thought, tree-of-thought). Others just iterate implicitly.

A key point for RSEs: the LLM itself is stateless — all state lives in the context window. The "loop" is managed by the surrounding application code, not the model.
:::

## Further Reading
Foundational concepts and Transformers

- 3Blue1Brown: [Neural Networks playlist](https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLZHQObOWTQDNU6R1_67000Dx_ZCJB-3pi) [@3blue1brown_nn]
- Andrej Karpathy: [Neural Networks: Zero to Hero](https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLAqhIrjkxbuWI23v9cThsA9GvCAUhRvKZ) [@karpathy_zero_to_hero]

- Stanford CS25: [Transformers United](https://web.stanford.edu/class/cs25/) [@stanford_cs25]
- DeepLearning.AI: [Build and Train an LLM with JAX](https://learn.deeplearning.ai/courses/build-and-train-an-llm-with-jax/lesson/gy364z/introduction) [@deeplearningai_llm_jax]

## Further Reading
Reinforcement Learning & Alignment

- Hugging Face: [Deep RL Course](https://huggingface.co/learn/deep-rl-course) [@huggingface_deep_rl]
- OpenAI: [Spinning Up in Deep RL](https://spinningup.openai.com) [@openai_spinningup]
